coredata.dc:title: Shape- and size-tunable synthesis of tin sulfide thin films for energy applications by electrodeposition
coredata.dc:creator: Vikraman, Dhanasekaran
Thiagarajan, Shrividhya
Karuppasamy, K.
Sanmugam, Anandhavelu
Choi, Jong-Hyeok
Prasanna, K.
Maiyalagan, T.
Thaiyan, Mahalingam
Kim, Hyun-Seok
coredata.dc:description: Size and shape tunable tin sulfide (SnS) thin film structures are successfully prepared by a simple cost-effective electrodeposition route. Scanning electron micrographs (SEM) effectively demonstrated the SnS shape modification. An ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) electrolyte was successfully used to alter the size of SnS. The SEM results also give evidence of the surface modification of SnS which was prepared with EDTA. Atomic force micrographs established the topological variations of SnS. Energy dispersive X-ray results confirmed the stoichiometric composition SnS prepared with and without EDTA. X-ray diffraction results revealed the polycrystalline orthorhombic structure of the SnS thin film. The optical band gap derived from the Tauc's plot was found to be in the 1.23–1.26 eV range. The near band edge emission peak for SnS was observed using photoluminescence properties. This simple strategy to synthesize a smooth, dense-packed and crack-free morphology could be an attractive way to produce SnS as a capable material for energy harvesting and optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface modification
Structure
SnS
XPS
Band gap
SEM
AFM

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20174030201520 ), and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (No. 2017R1D1A1A09000823 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of SnS thin films produced at different solution pH (a) 1.5 ± 0.1; (b) 2.5 ± 0.1 and 3.5 ± 0.1 using deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE and SnCl2: 20 mM.

SEM images of SnS thin films. Different deposition potential using solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 and SnCl2: 20 mM (a) −700 and (b) −850 mV vs SCE; different concentration of tin chloride using deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE and solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 (c) 10 mM and (d) 30 mM.
SEM images of SnS thin films by electrodeposition using a supporting EDTA electrolyte (a–b) 5 mM and (c–d) 20 mM (deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE, solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 and SnCl2: 20 mM).

AFM images of SnS thin films by electrodeposition using (a–b) without and with supporting electrolyte of EDTA (c–d) 5 mM and (e–f) 20 mM (deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE, solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 and SnCl2: 20 mM).

TEM images of SnS thin films by electrodeposition using (a–b) without and with supporting electrolyte of EDTA (c–d) 5 mM and (e–f) 20 mM (deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE, solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 and SnCl2: 20 mM). Inset - FFT pattern of corresponding images.

EDX profiles of SnS thin films by electrodeposition using (a) without and with supporting electrolyte of EDTA (a) 5 mM and (b) 20 mM (deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE, solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 and SnCl2: 20 mM).

(a) XRD and (b) Raman spectra of SnS thin film by electrodeposition using without and with supporting electrolyte of EDTA (deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE, solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 and SnCl2: 20 mM).

XPS (a) S 2p and (b) Sn 3d binding energy spectra of SnS thin film by electrodeposition using without and with supporting electrolyte of EDTA (deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE, solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 and SnCl2: 20 mM).

(a) Transmission, (b) Tauc's plot and (c) PL spectra of SnS thin film by electrodeposition using without and with supporting electrolyte of EDTA (deposition potential: −1000 mV vs SCE, solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1 and SnCl2: 20 mM).
Crystallite size and optical parameters of SnS thin films by electrodeposition using without and with supporting electrolyte of EDTA.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Potential: −1000 mV vs SCE, solution pH: 2.5 ± 0.1, SnCl2: 20 mM.

head.ce:title: Shape- and size-tunable synthesis of tin sulfide thin films for energy applications by electrodeposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Electronics and Electrical Engineering, Dongguk University-Seoul, Seoul 04620, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Electronics and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Conversion and Storage, Technical University of Denmark, Frederiksborgvej 399, P.O. Box 49, DK-4000 Roskilde, Denmark

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemical Energy Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur 603203, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630003, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Size and shape tunable tin sulfide (SnS) thin film structures are successfully prepared by a simple cost-effective electrodeposition route. Scanning electron micrographs (SEM) effectively demonstrated the SnS shape modification. An ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) electrolyte was successfully used to alter the size of SnS. The SEM results also give evidence of the surface modification of SnS which was prepared with EDTA. Atomic force micrographs established the topological variations of SnS. Energy dispersive X-ray results confirmed the stoichiometric composition SnS prepared with and without EDTA. X-ray diffraction results revealed the polycrystalline orthorhombic structure of the SnS thin film. The optical band gap derived from the Tauc's plot was found to be in the 1.23–1.26 eV range. The near band edge emission peak for SnS was observed using photoluminescence properties. This simple strategy to synthesize a smooth, dense-packed and crack-free morphology could be an attractive way to produce SnS as a capable material for energy harvesting and optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, materials research has been focused on the design and production of particles with a controlled size and shape by a simple and economical route, which is technologically significant for the vital problems being addressed by modern material science [1–4]. Since the optical, electronic and physicochemical properties are effectively tuned by the shape and size of nanostructures, several efforts have attempted to synthesize various inorganic materials to enhance their efficiency in various applications [5–11]. Nanostructured metal sulfides with unique electrical, structural and optical properties are auspicious materials for various uses such as energy, biomedicine, and optoelectronics [12–18]. Thus, the synthesis of metal sulfides with a controlled shape, untainted phase, and tunable electrical and optical properties are vital for industrial uses [19–24].

Tin sulfide, from the group of IV-VI elements, is a scientifically crucial material that has various oxidation states, such as SnS2, Sn4S5, SnS, Sn2S3, and Sn3S4 [14,19,25]. The chemical stability, natural abundance, non-toxicity, and easy and low-cost synthesis of SnS makes it suitable for the current needs of global energy research, especially for solar cells [26–28]. SnS has the distorted NaCl structure, which is orthorhombic (Pnma, D 2h
16) with an octahedral coordination geometry [29,30]. Typically, it has a GeS behavior by its bonding nature and coordination, hence SnS is capable of forming a morphology with a two-dimensional structure, such as plates, belts, and sheets, due to its lattice atom arrangement [31,32]. Moreover, SnS has received great attention due to its natural occurrence as Herzenbergite mineral which consists of solid SnS bonds in a layered structure within a wrinkled a–b plane with weak van der Walls forces between the layers, which is analogous to isoelectronic black phosphorus [33–35]. Due to these facts, SnS has high robustness, both environmentally and chemically [36]. In addition, it offers p-type behavior with the direct (~1.3 eV) and indirect (~1 eV) band gap, which reveals superior hole migration with a high absorption coefficient [12,37]. Theoretical estimations also show that SnS has the property to be an effective absorber of the solar spectrum, which suggests a use for green energy production in various optoelectronics and other devices [38,39]. Different established strategies were employed to fabricate SnS thin films, such as E-beam evaporation [40], atomic layer deposition [41], electrochemical deposition [31,42], thermal evaporation [43], spray pyrolysis [44], co-evaporation [45] and dip deposition [46]. Among them, electrodeposition is one of the most attractive methods, since it provides the merits of precise control of film deposition, ease of handling, and economy. In an earlier study, Liu et al. [47] reported on the size-selective synthesis of SnS nanostructures by spin coating for solar cell applications. Herein, shape and size-controlled synthesis of SnS thin film is successfully demonstrated by variation of electrodeposition parameters and the addition of a supporting electrolyte (EDTA). The detailed investigations were carried out for SnS morphological variations by scanning electron microscopy and atomic force microscopy.

Electrodeposition was employed with potentiostatic mode using a Princeton Applied Research EG&G, 362A, USA to prepare the SnS thin films. The electrodeposition was performed using an electrolyte comprised of sodium thiosulphate (Na2S2O3) and tin chloride (SnCl2). A conventional three-electrode setup, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) (reference electrode), graphite rod (counter electrode), and indium doped tin oxide (ITO) (working electrode), was used for the electrosynthesis of SnS thin films. The film was prepared by adjusting deposition parameters, such as solution pH (1.5 to 3.0 ± 0.1), deposition potential (−800 mV to −1000 mV vs SCE), and SnCl2 molar concentrations (10 to 30 mM). The pH value was tuned by the addition of diluted H2SO4 solution. The deposition conditions were optimized for yielding pinhole free smooth thin films with stoichiometric compositions. Under the optimized conditions, the supporting electrolyte (EDTA) was mixed into the electrolyte solution at 5 and 20 mM. The electrolytic coating time and bath temperature were fixed at 30 mins and 90 °C.

Morphological variations were captured using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Philips, XL 30, The Netherlands) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL-2010F). The surface topological modifications were observed by atomic force microscope (AFM) (Nanoscope E–3138J). Structural variations were characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (X'PERT PRO PANalytical, The Netherlands), with Cukα radiation (λ = 0.1540 nm). A Renishaw Raman microscope (RE04) was employed to analyze Raman scattering analysis. PHI 5000 Versa Probe II, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to identify the structure composition. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 9) was employed to examine the optical properties. A Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer was used to investigate the luminescence behavior of SnS thin films at room temperature using an Xe laser source with the excitation wavelength of 500 nm. Stylus surface profilometer (Mitutoyo SJ-301) was used to measure the film thickness.

The electrochemical growth kinetics were derived for SnS thin film growth by electrodeposition from an aqueous electrolytic solution comprising SnCl2 and Na2S2O3. The following electrochemical derivations are for the co-deposition of S and Sn:
(1)
Sn
2 +
+ 2
e −
→ Sn
E 0
= 0.150 V
E Sn
=
E 0
+
RT
2 F
ln
a
Sn
2 +
a Sn
E Sn
= 0.150 +
0.591 2
ln
a
Sn
2 +
a Sn
(2)
H 2
SO 3
+ 4
H +
+ 4
H −
→ S + 3
H 2
O
E 0
= 0.449 V
E 0
= E
0 S
+
RT
4 F
ln
a
S 2
O 3
a s
+
RT
4 F
ln
a H +
4
E 0
= 0.449 +
0.591 4
ln
a
S 2
O 3
a s
+
0.591 4
ln
a H +
4
whereE
S
0 and E
Sn
0 are the standard electrode potential for S and Sn, respectively, in terms of SCE. a
Sn
2+ and a
Sn are the Sn ion activities in the electrolyte and deposited film, respectively. Sn deposition proceeds at a positive potential compared with their standard reduction potential by receiving free energy in the formation of SnS as given in Eq. (3):
(3)
Sn
2 +
+ S + 2
e −
= SnS

Shape and size selective coatings of SnS thin films were successfully established by electrosynthesis. SEM studies plausibly explained the variation of grain sizes and shape with changes in deposition conditions. Solution pH is a crucial factor to alter the film deposition kinetics in electrodeposition; hence, different solution pH values, from 1.5 to 3.5 ± 0.1, were used to deposit SnS thin films. Fig. 1 shows SEM micrographs of SnS thin films prepared using different solution pHs. Different sizes of pyramidal shaped grains were observed for low solution pH value (1.5 ± 0.1), as shown in Fig. 1a. When the SnS thin film was prepared using 2.5 ± 0.1 solution pH, the SEM micrograph revealed that a number of stacked nano-layers making up the pyramidal growth tend to avoid sharp edges. The tips tend to flatten, and the triangular cross-section is visible near the edges. The observed grain size is enhanced owing to the coalescence of smaller nano-grains into sizeable grains. This results in an improvement in seeding the over-growth, and the coatings produce a more compact and homogeneous morphology. Upon further increasing the solution pH to 3.5 ± 0.1, larger size grains are exhibited on the surface of the SnS thin film. The increase of grain sizes as solution pH increases is pictured in Fig. 1.

The surface modification is demonstrated by adjusting deposition potential (−700 to −1000 mV vs SCE) for SnS thin film preparation with a 2.5 ± 0.1 solution pH. Fig. 2a–b show the SEM images for SnS thin films prepared using −700, and −850 mV vs SCE deposition potential, respectively. Polygonal-shaped grains are observed in the SnS film surface for −700 mV vs SCE deposition potential (Fig. 2a). Upon further decreasing the deposition potential to −850 mV vs SCE, cuboid-shaped polygonal structured grains are clearly observed with nano-wire shaped background grains. For the SnS prepared using −1000 mV vs SCE deposition potential (Fig. 1b), stacked nano-layers with pyramidal shapes are exhibited as explained earlier. Similarly, the role of SnCl2 electrolyte concentration on the surface modification is examined using 10 and 30 mM concentrations with a 2.5 ± 0.1 solution pH and a −1000 mV deposition potential vs SCE. Fig. 2c–d display the SEM images of SnS thin films with different SnCl2 concentrations. For the SnS film deposited using 10 mM SnCl2, the surface of the film exhibited a morphology resembling triangular pyramid-shaped grains (Fig. 2c). The well-defined shapes and their gain boundaries are observed clearly. A typical SEM image for SnS thin film with 20 mM of SnCl2 is Fig. 1b. Upon further increasing the SnCl2 concentration to 30 mM for SnS preparation (Fig. 2d), dissolute grains shapes are observed due to the high amount of Sn ions separation. Hence, 2.5 ± 0.1 solution pH, −1000 mV deposition potential vs SCE and 20 mM SnCl2 were fixed as optimized growth parameters for further processes.

In addition, the supporting EDTA electrolyte was mixed into the electrolytic bath and its role was studied in SnS film formation and surface modification. Two different concentrations, 5 and 20 mM, were used along with the optimized deposition parameters. Fig. 3 shows the SEM images for SnS thin films prepared using EDTA. For a SnS thin film prepared with 5 mM EDTA, the film is made up of nanosheet shaped grains and closely distributed over the surface (Fig. 3a–b). The morphology in this film is due to the dispersion of stacked layers that formed through agglomeration without EDTA, which suggests the strong role of 5 mM EDTA to bypass the agglomeration process during electrodeposition on the film surface. Chopped smaller grains with different domain shapes were observed for SnS film that was prepared using 20 mM EDTA (Fig. 3c–d). These might have been formed by the clustering of adatoms uniformly with minimum energy transaction. The SEM results illustrated that the SnS thin film grown with a higher concentration of EDTA has a uniform morphology without pinholes or cracks and regularly deposited on the substrate. Smaller sized nanograins homogenously covered over the flat background may be due to the tendency to form an amorphous phase. The film thicknesses are varied slightly with electrodeposition parameters which were estimated by surface profilometer and their results are provided in the Supporting information Fig. S1. The optimized conditions prepared SnS film without EDTA has 850 nm thickness, whereas it has decreased to 840 and 828 nm for 5 and 20 mM EDTA using prepared SnS, respectively. This variation is observed due to reduction of reaction rate by the alternation of bath solution complexation behavior [48].

Topological AFM images further confirmed the observed surface modification for SnS thin films which were prepared using optimized parameters (hereafter referred to as without EDTA) and with 5 and 20 mM EDTA. Larger size grains are observed in the AFM image for an optimized SnS thin film (Fig. 4a) and the enlarged image (Fig. 4b) confirmed their presence. A nanosheet-like topology is observed for a SnS thin film which was prepared using 5 mM EDTA (Fig. 4c–d). The domains of grains with nano-sized grains are confirmed by the AFM topographs for SnS which was prepared using 20 mM EDTA, as shown in Fig. 4e–f. To confirm the nanostructures, TEM investigations were carried out for without and with EDTA using SnS thin films. Fig. 5a–b show the TEM images of optimized condition using prepared SnS film. Nanosized grains are observed with different shapes such as cuboid, spherical and their elongated cylinder like grains as shown in Fig. 5a. Higher magnification TEM image (Fig. 5b) with their inset FFT pattern clearly represents grain shapes in electrodeposited SnS. For 5 mM EDTA using electrodeposited SnS, the rod like grains are exhibited with spherical and cuboid shapes (Fig. 5c–d). When the SnS film electrodeposited using 20 mM EDTA, highly dispersed spherically shaped grains are observed as shown in Fig. 5e which is confirmed in previous SEM and AFM results. The higher magnification TEM image is further proved their shape in the Fig. 5f and their corresponding FFT pattern is inserted. The compositional study was carried out with energy dispersive analysis by X-ray (EDX). Fig. 6a–c show the EDX profiles of SnS thin films with and without EDTA (5 and 20 mM). The optimized film contains 52:48 atomic percentage for Sn:S. The observed EDX profiles confirmed the presence of Sn and S without any major variation due to the addition of EDTA.

XRD, Raman, and XPS structural studies were performed to confirm the development of SnS film. Fig. 7a displays the XRD patterns of SnS thin films electrosynthesized with and without EDTA (5 and 20 mM). The perceived diffraction patterns are confirmed by a standard result (JCPDS: 75-1225). The XRD patterns revealed the polycrystalline nature of SnS films with an orthorhombic structure. The observed diffraction peaks, for SnS prepared without EDTA, are at 2θ 26.28°, 27.03°, 30.23°, 31.18°, 32.18°, 35.13°, 39.53°, 44.03°, and 45.53° corresponding to the (201), (210), (011), (111), (400), (211), (311), (202), and (020) lattice planes, respectively. The observed pattern confirmed that the (111) lattice plane is preferentially orientated. From the XRD results, the supporting EDTA electrolyte affects the polycrystalline nature of a SnS thin film significantly. The XRD patterns show three sharp peaks at 28.53°, 30.13°, 35.08°, 50.53°, and 60.28° for (210), (011), (211), (212), and (421) orthorhombic directions, respectively for SnS thin films prepared using EDTA, with (211) as a preferential orientation. The sharpness and intensity of the (011) peak are decreased for higher EDTA concentration. The introduction of EDTA encourages the dispersion of nanocrystallites in electrodeposited SnS thin films, which makes a variation in morphology as discussed earlier. Ghazali et al. [49] reported the role of EDTA in the nucleation and controllability of reaction kinetics. The controlled reaction kinetics is also given the role to tune the film thickness and uniformity, which can be established by the available number of coordination sites and nucleation sites at the electrode surface [50]. Our studies reveal that the addition of EDTA not only provides better reaction kinetics but also different agglomeration possibilities depending on the initial nucleation.

In addition, XRD patterns for SnS thin films prepared using different solution pHs, deposition potentials, and SnCl2 concentrations are provided in Figs. S2–S4 (Supporting information), respectively. From the displayed XRD patterns, electrodeposited SnS thin films were grown in a unique orthorhombic structure with a polycrystalline nature without any impurities. The crystallite size (D) of SnS thin films were extracted by the procedure using full-width at half-maximum intensity (FWHM):
(4)
D =
0.9 λ
β cos θ
where λ is the wavelength, D is the crystallite size and β is FWHM. The estimated crystallite size value is 45 nm for SnS thin films prepared without EDTA. The crystallite size decreases for the SnS thin films prepared using EDTA and found to be 29 and 25 nm for 5 and 20 mM EDTA, respectively.

Raman scattering further confirmed the SnS formation. Fig. 7b shows Raman spectra of SnS thin films prepared with and without EDTA. From the observed Raman profiles, SnS has emitted two Raman active modes, the Ag mode and B3g mode [51,52]. Ag mode Raman peaks are observed at 93, 201 and 229 cm−1, whereas the B3g mode is exhibited at 166 cm−1. In addition, the observed Raman positions are slightly varied for SnS thin films prepared using EDTA. XPS analyses were performed for SnS thin films prepared with and without EDTA. Fig. 8a–b show the S 2p and Sn 3d binding energy XPS profiles, respectively. The XPS core profile of S 2p shows a peak at 161.3 and 163.3 eV binding energy, which is related to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 8a). The Sn 3d profile (Fig. 8b) shows peaks at 495 and 486 eV that correspond to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively. The observed peak separation energy of Sn 3d is 9 eV. which is consistent with an earlier report [53]. Hence, the observed Raman and XPS results are further confirmation of SnS formation.

The characteristic optical behavior of SnS thin films is demonstrated by transmission spectra, as shown in Fig. 9a. The transmittance spectrum was obtained for SnS films that were prepared by electrodeposition with and without EDTA as a supporting electrolyte. The observation surface plasmon resonance in the transmittance spectra confirms the nanometer range of crystallite size. The transmittance rises considerably for SnS films prepared using EDTA. This observation is correlated with the surface modification of SnS films prepared using EDTA, as explained in the SEM discussion. The optical phonons transition is estimated using Eq. (5) [54]:
(5)
αhν = A
hν −
E g
n
where hν is a phonon energy, α is an absorption coefficient, Eg is an energy gap, n is an integer of electronic transitions, and A is an energy-dependent constant. For semiconductors, n = 1/2 for direct transitions and n = 2 for indirect transitions. Tauc's plot of SnS thin films is provided in Fig. 9b. The extracted band gap values are provided in Table 1 . The energy band gap is found to be in the 1.23–1.26 eV range. The band gap value enhances for the SnS prepared using EDTA, which may be due to the decrease of crystallite size and the increase of strain values. The linearly fitted slope values are at 2.49 × 1014, 1.97 × 1014 and 1.91 × 1014 cm−2·(hν)2 for without EDTA and 5 and 20 mM EDTA using prepared SnS, respectively.

The optical transmittance values were used to estimate the refractive index and extinction coefficient using a modified Swanepoel's procedure [55–57]. The extracted refractive index and extinction coefficient values for SnS films are presented in Table 1. The refractive index is found to decrease for SnS prepared using EDTA, which may be due to the increase of transmittance behavior and improved surface behavior with clear grain boundaries [57]. The extinction coefficient value is increased due to the variation of absorption coefficient values. The luminescence behavior was characterized by photoluminescence (PL) as shown in Fig. 9c. The 550 nm excitation wavelength was used to perceive the PL spectra. A broad peak observed at around 750 nm, which is related to the band energy of SnS film prepared without EDTA [58]. In addition, a slight hump is exhibited around 650 nm, which may be due to observed defects on the surface of SnS. A sharp and intense peak emerged at 715 and 700 nm for SnS films prepared using 5 and 20 mM of EDTA, respectively without any defects related peak. From the observed results, EDTA is the crucial factor to reduce the size of the SnS grains without agglomeration, which would be vital for 2D layered materials especially in electrochemical energy related applications. In addition, SnS films with larger size grains are helpful to improve photovoltaics based applications. Hence, this work discussed the shape and size selective synthesis of SnS for various energy related applications, and it creates a new platform to produce SnS films and their hybrid materials for future energy harvesting uses.

The shape selective synthesis of SnS thin films was successfully demonstrated by electrodeposition with the tuning of their deposition parameters, such as solution pH, deposition potential, and Sn source concentration. The effect of a supporting electrolyte (EDTA) on the morphological, structural, and optical properties was studied. XRD patterns revealed the distorted NaCl structure of orthorhombic structured SnS in all the films. SEM images revealed the nano-sheets and nano-domains for SnS film surfaces prepared using EDTA. AFM topological images displayed the observed surface modification of SnS with and without EDTA. The band gap values were observed in the 1.23–1.26 eV range. Hence, the shape and size selective synthesis of SnS by electrosynthesis is established and it would be an auspicious candidate for various energy harvesting uses.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20174030201520), and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (No. 2017R1D1A1A09000823).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.02.056.
